title: Joseph Gerrish (1788-1864): From Lebanon in the Maine Piscataqua Country to Sharon, Massachusetts
narrative: Joseph Gerrish was born on 27 February 1788 in Lebanon, York County, Maine, the son of John Gerrish and Elizabeth Warren. This is not a reconstructed or inferred date: it is set down word for word in the published Vital Records of Lebanon, Maine, to the Year 1892, whose births volume records 'Joseph, s. John and Elizabeth (Warren), Feb. 27, 1788' from the town register (cited there as T.R.3:301). The same volume independently confirms both parents - 'John, h. Elizabeth (Warren), Feb. 4, 1761' and 'Elizabeth, w. John Gerrish, Aug. 17, 1764' - and lists Joseph among a sizable family of children born to that couple. His documented siblings include Samuel (1785), Nathaniel (1790), James Jr. (1793), George (1795), Timothy (1799), John (1801), Elisha (1805), and Eliza (1810). His birth date thus rests on a solid, transcribed primary record rather than on family tradition.

Lebanon sits in the inland reaches of York County in the old Piscataqua country - the borderland of the Salmon Falls and Piscataqua rivers dividing Maine from New Hampshire, the region anchored by Portsmouth and Dover. The Gerrish name is one of the genuinely old and well-documented surnames of that region, and the Lebanon records themselves preserve the family's pedigree. One Gerrish entry in the town's vital records explicitly traces the line to 'Capt. William Gerrish settled in this Country, 1639,' and names the intervening generations: 'Capt. John, h. Elizabeth (Waldron), (d. of Col. ... of Dover, N.H.), s. of Capt. William, 1645.' Captain William Gerrish (born in England about 1617, died 1687) was an early and prominent settler of Newbury, Massachusetts, and the ancestor of the large Gerrish family of New England; his son Captain John Gerrish (born 1646) married Elizabeth Waldron, daughter of Major Richard Waldron of Dover, and settled on the Piscataqua. That this Lebanon family understood itself as a branch of the Newbury-Dover Gerrishes is therefore documented in its own town records. Exactly how Joseph's father John connects, generation by generation, to Captain William is not spelled out in the free sources consulted here, so the broad descent is reported as the records state it, without asserting a precise unbroken pedigree that the sources do not prove.

Joseph married a wife named Lydia, and the two share the Gerrish surname - he by birth, she at least by married name as recorded. The Lebanon births volume documents a cluster of children of 'Joseph and Lydia' entered on the same town-register page (T.R.3:305) as Joseph's own family: Hiram (10 March 1815), Hannah (28 August 1819), Sarah Ann (27 July 1824), Jonathan H. (27 April 1827), and Elfonza (23 June 1830), with a son Levi (8 August 1821) entered as 'Joseph and Hannah' in what appears to be a clerk's slip for the same household. These records place Joseph and Lydia raising a family in Lebanon from the mid-1810s through about 1830. Whether Lydia was herself a born Gerrish - a cousin or more distant kinswoman within this sprawling Piscataqua clan - or whether she simply carried the same surname by coincidence or through her own marriage record, is not established by the free sources examined here and is honestly left open rather than guessed at.

The family connection toward the Cromwell line runs, by the tradition this deep-dive was asked to trace, through a Hannah Gerrish who married Simon Cromwell. It is worth noting that Joseph and Lydia's documented children include a daughter Hannah, born in Lebanon on 28 August 1819, which makes a Hannah Gerrish of the right name and generation demonstrably present in this household. That said, no record located in the free sources here - neither the Lebanon vital records nor the searched genealogies - directly documents a marriage of any Hannah Gerrish to a Simon Cromwell. The Cromwell link is therefore reported as the family's claimed connection rather than as a verified one; the most that the documents independently support is that a daughter named Hannah Gerrish existed in exactly the place and era the tradition requires.

Joseph's life spanned a transformative era in northern New England. Born in 1788, in the first year under the new federal Constitution, he grew up in an inland Maine farm town when Maine was still the northern district of Massachusetts; he was thirty-two when Maine became a separate state in 1820. The interior York County of his childhood was a country of family farms, woodlots, sawmills, and tightly intermarried neighborhoods - the Gerrishes, Blaisdells, Wentworths, Goodwins, and their kin recur again and again across Lebanon's pages, often marrying one another. Like many of his Maine-born generation, Joseph eventually left the land of his birth: he removed south into Massachusetts, settling in Sharon, in Norfolk County, on the rural fringe between Boston and Providence. Such a Maine-to-Massachusetts migration was entirely typical of the antebellum decades, as the older inland New England farm towns shed people toward the growing towns and industrial districts of eastern Massachusetts. The precise year and circumstances of Joseph's own move are not pinned down in the free records consulted, so the relocation is reported as fact - his death place confirms it - while its timing is left honestly uncertain.

Joseph Gerrish died on 10 April 1864 in Sharon, Norfolk County, Massachusetts, in the third spring of the Civil War. He was seventy-six. By then the New England he had been born into - rural, agrarian, and bounded by the Piscataqua - had given way to a nation at war over slavery and union, and a man of his age would have lived the war as an elderly civilian on the home front rather than as a soldier. Massachusetts had begun statewide registration of deaths in 1841, so an official 1864 death record for Sharon would lie in the state's vital records; the specifics of that entry were not retrieved from the free sources here, and the death date is reported as the established fact given for this person rather than re-derived. What can be said with documentary confidence is the spine of his life: born 27 February 1788 in Lebanon, York County, Maine, son of John Gerrish and Elizabeth Warren, of the old Piscataqua Gerrish family that traced itself to Captain William Gerrish of Newbury; husband of Lydia and father of a Lebanon-born family including a daughter Hannah; and a Maine emigrant who ended his long life in Sharon, Massachusetts, in 1864. The wider texture - the Gerrish clan's deep New England roots, the Maine-to-Massachusetts migration of his generation, and the Civil War that framed his final years - is drawn from the documented history of his family and region and offered as honest context, while the threads that the free record cannot confirm, above all the Lydia kinship and the Cromwell marriage, are flagged as open rather than invented.

sources.title: Vital Records of Lebanon, Maine, to the Year 1892, Vol. I - Births (Maine Historical Society, 1922) - birth of 'Joseph, s. John and Elizabeth (Warren), Feb. 27, 1788'; parents John Gerrish & Elizabeth Warren; siblings; children of Joseph & Lydia; Capt. William/Capt. John Gerrish pedigree note
Vital Records of Lebanon, Maine, to the Year 1892, Vol. II - Marriages (Maine Historical Society) - Gerrish-family marriages of Lebanon
Capt. William Gerrish (1617-1687) of Newbury, Massachusetts - founding ancestor of the New England Gerrish family; son Capt. John Gerrish married Elizabeth Waldron of Dover, N.H.
Lebanon, Maine - Wikipedia (town in York County, in the inland Piscataqua/Salmon Falls region of southern Maine)
Sharon, Massachusetts - Wikipedia (town in Norfolk County between Boston and Providence; Joseph Gerrish's place of death, 1864)
Piscataqua River - Wikipedia (context for the Portsmouth/Dover/York County borderland where the Gerrish family was rooted)

images.caption: The Ole Bull Residence in West Lebanon, Maine - a surviving 19th-century house in the inland York County town where Joseph Gerrish was born in 1788. Contextual view of Joseph's birthplace; not a depiction of Joseph or his own home. (Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons.)
